analysis.briefing_paragraph:
Harbour Town is one of those courses that just does not care how far you hit it. At roughly 7,100 yards playing to a par 71, it's among the shortest venues on tour, and the distance multiplier here is only partial — meaning the bombers get almost zero advantage and in some cases get actively punished for pulling driver on holes that funnel into tight tree-lined corridors and those iconic railroad tie-bordered greens. The course type is TIGHT, and that designation matters more here than almost anywhere else on the schedule. The 62.5% course average driving accuracy number tells the story — if you're missing fairways at Harbour Town, you're hitting wedges and short irons from pine straw and angles that turn easy scoring holes into par scrambles. The dominant stat historically aligns with approach play (APP) — specifically the ability to flight irons precisely from tight lies and manufacture the right angles into small, firm, contoured greens. Ball-striking quality is the engine here, full stop. The putter can win you the tournament on the weekend, but it won't carry you into contention if your iron play is leaking. The big narrative entering this week is impossible to ignore: Rory McIlroy just defended his Masters title, becoming only the fourth player in history to do so. That's a massive emotional and physical storyline. The question isn't whether Rory belongs here — he absolutely fits Harbour Town's precision profile — it's whether he shows up at all and if so, what version of him shows up four days after one of the most draining weeks in golf. Masters hangover is real, but so is Masters momentum. Beyond Rory, we're setting up for the usual Harbour Town dynamic: the course rewards a certain type of tidy, controlled ball-striker, which means the field tends to tighten up in terms of realistic contenders. The bombers who feasted at Augusta on length are suddenly neutralized. Weather data is unavailable at this stage — Hilton Head in April can introduce coastal wind that makes the precision demand even more severe, which historically suppresses scoring and makes the course play even more like a precision test. That context matters for wave assignment and FRL targeting when we get there. ---

[Claude] ## HARBOUR TOWN — WHAT WINS HERE (Framework Anchor)
[Claude] Before I touch a single price, let me re-establish what the course demands, because that's what determines whether a price is fair.
[Claude] **Harbour Town is a precision test, not a power test.** It's one of the shortest tracks on tour. The premium is on:
[Claude] - **Approach Play (APP)** — small, firm, elevated greens demand precise distance control and trajectory
[Claude] - **Driving Accuracy (OTT)** — not distance, *accuracy*. Trees and tight corridors punish crooked drivers
[Claude] - **Around the Green / Short Game** — scoring zones are tight, par saves matter
[Claude] - **Putting on Bentgrass** — the one track where putting variance matters a bit more than usual because the greens are readable and consistent, rewarding elite putters

[Claude] **Ball-striking is still the engine here, but the profile shifts toward control over power.** Big hitters who can't shape it or hit fairways get exposed. Accurate, approach-dominant players with functional putting are the archetypal Harbour Town winner.
[Claude] Past winners profile: Dustin Johnson (accuracy phase), Bryson (control year), Stewart Cink, Jim Furyk repeatedly — this course loves precision.
